title: Volunteer Handbook
body:
Annapolis Library Volunteer Handbook

This is the home page for volunteers at the Annapolis Library. It gathers the practices we ask everyone to follow at the front desk, in the computer lab, and in the tutoring room. If something here is out of date, leave a comment and Gloria or I will update it.

Hours we are typically staffed by volunteers: Tuesday and Thursday mornings, and Saturday mid-day. Sign in at the desk binder when you arrive and sign out when you leave so we have an accurate count for the county report.

Child sections cover onboarding, the tutoring program, the computer lab, reading-list suggestions, and author-talk planning. Start with the onboarding checklist if you are new.

title: Volunteer onboarding checklist
body:
Volunteer onboarding checklist

For a new volunteer's first two weeks. Gloria handles the paperwork; the rest of us walk you through the floor.

  1. Complete the county volunteer form and background acknowledgement with Gloria before your first shift.
  2. Get your name badge and the desk binder overview.
  3. Shadow one full morning shift at the front desk before working it alone.
  4. Learn the sign-in binder, the phone, and where the hold shelf is.
  5. Walk through the computer-lab basics: turning stations on, the print release, and who to call when a machine locks up.
  6. If you plan to tutor, read the GED tutoring session guide and sit in on one session first.

Questions are welcome any time. No one is expected to know all of this in the first week.

title: GED tutoring session guide
body:
GED tutoring session guide

We tutor on Thursday mornings, 10:00 to 12:00, in the small meeting room. Most students are working toward the reasoning-through-language-arts and math sections. This guide is meant to keep our approach steady from one volunteer to the next.

A few things that have served me well over the years: start each session by asking what the student wants to get done, not what we planned to cover. Work one topic at a time and let them do the writing or the arithmetic themselves. When a student is stuck, it is almost always worth slowing down rather than supplying the answer.

Keep a short note at the end of each session on what was covered so the next volunteer can pick up the thread. The notes binder lives on the tutoring-room shelf. If a student misses two Thursdays in a row, let Gloria know so she can check in.

title: GED math: fractions and percentages refresher
body:
GED math: fractions and percentages refresher

A working page for the topics students ask about most. It is not a curriculum, just the explanations and practice problems that tend to land.

Fractions: I find it helps to tie every fraction back to something concrete before we touch the rules. Percentages follow naturally once a student is comfortable moving between a fraction and a decimal.

There is a set of practice problems in the shared folder, sorted from gentle to harder. Please add any problem you found useful, and note which ones caused confusion so we can rework them.

title: Reading-list recommendations for adult learners
body:
Reading-list recommendations for adult learners

Titles that have worked well for students building reading stamina before the language-arts section. These lean toward short chapters, clear prose, and subjects adults find worth their time.

I have leaned on narrative nonfiction and shorter novels rather than anything that feels like a textbook. The library keeps most of these on the shelf, and the ones that are out can usually be reserved through the catalog. If a student finishes one and wants more in the same vein, ask at the desk and we can usually point them somewhere.

Add your own suggestions below, with a line on who it suited. A list is only useful if it reflects what actually held a reader's attention.

title: Computer-lab help notes
body:
Computer-lab help notes

The most common things patrons need help with in the lab, and how to handle them without needing staff every time.

Printing: the release station is the small screen by the door; jobs clear after an hour. Resetting a forgotten catalog password is done at the desk, not in the lab. For anything to do with a patron's personal email or banking, we help them find the button but never type their password or handle their account for them.

If a station freezes, a restart fixes it most of the time. If a machine will not come back, put an out-of-order card on it and note the station number for the staff IT queue. Please do not try to reinstall anything.

title: Author-talk planning notes
body:
Author-talk planning notes

A running checklist for the evening author talks the library hosts. We usually run four or five a year. This covers the volunteer side; the branch manager handles the author contract and honorarium.

Two weeks out: confirm the room, chairs, and the microphone; post the notice on the community board and the newsletter; set aside copies of the author's book if we have them. The evening of: arrive an hour early to set up, staff a welcome table, and keep a headcount for the county report. Afterward, stack the chairs and leave the room as we found it.

The March talk on Chesapeake Bay history filled the room, so we may want to plan for overflow seating next time a local-history author comes through.

title: Front-desk quick reference
body:
Front-desk quick reference

The short answers to the questions that come up most at the desk, so a volunteer working alone can find them fast.

Loan periods, renewals, and holds: the standard loan is three weeks with two renewals unless there is a hold. New library cards need a photo ID and a current address; the form is in the desk drawer. Fine-free applies to most materials, but not to the hotspots and the museum passes, so check those back in promptly.

If you cannot answer something, it is always fine to say you will check and call a staff member over. Better that than a guess.
